CHAPTER 1
Foundation of Large-Scale Systems Design
1.1 What is System Design?
System design is the process of defining the architecture, components, modules, interfaces, and data flow
of a system to satisfy specified requirements. Large-scale systems must handle millions of users, petabytes of
data, and high concurrency while remaining reliable and maintainable.
 Examples of large-scale systems: Google Search (8.5 billion queries/day), Netflix (270M subscribers),
WhatsApp (100B messages/day).
1.2 System Design Principles
Scalability
The ability to handle increasing load by adding resources (scale up = bigger
machine; scale out = more machines).
Reliability
The system continues to work correctly even when faults occur. Measured by MTBF
(Mean Time Between Failures).
Availability
The fraction of time the system is operational. Formula: Availability = Uptime /
(Uptime + Downtime). 99.9% = ~8.7 hrs downtime/year.
Maintainability
How easy it is to fix bugs, add features, or operate the system. Achieved through
modularity, good documentation, and clean code.
Performance
How fast the system responds to requests (latency) and how much work it does per
unit time (throughput).
Fault Tolerance
The system's ability to continue operating despite partial failures by using
redundancy, replication, and failover.
Consistency
All nodes see the same data at the same time. Strong consistency is hard to achieve
in distributed systems.
Security
Protection against unauthorized access, data breaches, and denial-of-service
attacks.
1.3 Functional vs Non-Functional Requirements
 Aspect
 Functional Requirements
Non-Functional Requirements
Definition
WHAT the system should do
HOW WELL the system should do it

Examples

items, make a payment

uptime, handle 1M concurrent users
Testability

Verified by load testing,
benchmarking

1.4 Latency vs Throughput
Latency is the time taken to complete a single request (measured in ms or seconds). Throughput is the
number of requests processed per unit time (measured in req/sec or QPS — Queries Per Second).

hour

■ MUST KNOW: Latency numbers every engineer should know: L1 cache = 1ns, RAM = 100ns, SSD = 100
microseconds, HDD = 10ms, Network (same region) = 0.5ms, Network (cross-continent) = 150ms.

1.6 Architectural Patterns
Monolithic
All components in one deployable unit. Simple to develop initially, but hard to scale
and maintain as it grows. Example: Traditional e-commerce apps.
Microservices
Application split into small, independent services, each responsible for one business
capability. Each can be deployed, scaled, and updated independently. Example:
Netflix (700+ microservices).
5:
Advanced System Design  |  23CS020
Chitkara University    B.E. CSE 6th Sem
Page 5  |  Exam: 09 April 2026  |  60 Marks  |  60 Minutes
Event-Driven
Components communicate through events/messages. Decoupled, asynchronous.
Example: Kafka-based pipelines, real-time analytics.
Layered (N-Tier)
System divided into layers: Presentation, Business Logic, Data. Each layer talks only
to adjacent layers. Example: Traditional web apps.
CQRS
Command Query Responsibility Segregation — separate read and write paths for
scalability and performance.
Event Sourcing
Store all changes as a sequence of events, not current state. The current state is
derived by replaying events.
Service Mesh
Infrastructure layer handling service-to-service communication (load balancing,
service discovery, observability). Example: Istio, Linkerd.
■ KEY TAKEAWAYS
✓ Functional = what system does; Non-Functional = how well (availability, latency, throughput).
✓ Latency = single request time; Throughput = requests per second.
✓ Availability 99.9% = 8.7 hrs downtime/year; 99.99% = 52 min; 99.999% = 5 min.
✓ Monolith = single unit; Microservices = independent services; Event-Driven = async messages.
✓ Always gather BOTH types of requirements before designing.

CHAPTER 2
Fundamentals of Distributed Systems
2.1 Definition & Characteristics
A distributed system is a collection of independent computers that appears to its users as a single coherent
system. The computers communicate and coordinate only by passing messages over a network.
Leslie Lamport's famous quote: "A distributed system is one in which the failure of a computer you didn't even
know existed can render your own computer unusable."
Concurrency
Multiple components execute simultaneously. Requires careful coordination to avoid
race conditions and data inconsistency.
No Global Clock
Each machine has its own clock. There is no single global time — events can't be
ordered by wall-clock time alone. Requires logical clocks (Lamport, Vector).
Partial Failures
Some components can fail while others continue working. The system must detect
and handle partial failures gracefully.
Message Passing
Communication only happens through message passing over the network — which
can be slow, unreliable, or reordered.
Transparency
The system hides the complexity of distribution from users: access, location,
migration, replication, and failure transparency.
2.2 Advantages of Distributed Systems
 Scalability: Add more machines to handle growing load (horizontal scaling) — virtually unlimited capacity.
 Fault Tolerance: No single point of failure — if one node fails, others continue serving requests.
 Performance: Work can be parallelized across many machines; data can be served from geographically
closer nodes (reduced latency).
 Resource Sharing: Hardware, data, and software can be shared across the network.
 Availability: Replicated data and services ensure the system is always accessible.
2.3 Challenges of Distributed Systems
 Network Unreliability: Messages can be lost, delayed, duplicated, or arrive out of order.
 Partial Failures: Hard to distinguish a slow node from a crashed node.
 Consistency vs Availability: Maintaining data consistency across replicas while staying highly available is
fundamentally difficult (CAP Theorem).
 Security: Larger attack surface; messages can be intercepted.
 Complexity: Distributed systems are much harder to build, test, and debug than centralized systems.
 Clock Skew: No global clock — ordering events across nodes is non-trivial.
2.4 Types of Distributed Systems
 Type
 Description
 Examples

✓ Client-Server: centralized, server authoritative; P2P: decentralized, every node equal.
✓ Main challenges: network unreliability, partial failures, consistency vs availability.

CHAPTER 3
Distributed System Failures
3.1 Why Failures are Inevitable
In a large distributed system with 1,000 nodes, if each node has 99.9% uptime, the probability of ALL nodes
being up at the same time is 0.999^1000 = 36.8%. So failures are the norm, not the exception!
3.2 Failure Models
Crash Failure
A node simply stops working and does not respond to further messages. The
simplest failure model — the node is either working or completely stopped. Example:
Server power failure.
Omission Failure
A node fails to send or receive some messages. Send omission: node fails to send a
message. Receive omission: node fails to receive a message. Example: Network
packet drops.
Timing Failure
A node delivers responses outside the specified time bounds — too slow (late) or in
some cases too fast. Example: Slow database query causing timeout.
Byzantine Failure
The worst case — a node behaves arbitrarily and may send INCORRECT,
inconsistent, or malicious data to different nodes. Named after the Byzantine
Generals Problem. Example: Hacked node, bit-flip errors.
Network Partition
The network splits into two or more groups that cannot communicate with each other,
even though individual nodes are still functioning. Example: Data centre network
switch failure.
Fail-Stop Failure
A node crashes and others can DETECT the crash. Idealised version of crash failure
— easier to handle.
■ ORDER: Failure Severity Order (easiest to handle → hardest): Crash Stop < Crash < Omission < Timing <
Byzantine
3.3 Fault Tolerance Strategies
Redundancy
 Active Redundancy (Hot Standby): Multiple replicas process the same request simultaneously. If primary
fails, standby takes over immediately with zero downtime.
 Passive Redundancy (Warm Standby): Backup receives updates but is not processing live traffic. Takes
seconds to failover.
 Cold Standby: Backup only starts up when primary fails. Cheapest but longest failover time.
Replication
 Purpose: Store copies of data on multiple nodes to prevent data loss and allow reads from nearest replica.
 Synchronous Replication: Write is committed only after ALL replicas acknowledge. Strong consistency, but
higher latency.
9:
Advanced System Design  |  23CS020
Chitkara University    B.E. CSE 6th Sem
Page 9  |  Exam: 09 April 2026  |  60 Marks  |  60 Minutes
 Asynchronous Replication: Write is committed after primary acknowledges; replicas update eventually.
Lower latency, but risk of data loss.
Checkpointing
 Periodically save the state of the system so that on failure, it can restart from the last checkpoint instead of
from scratch.
Timeouts & Retries
 If a message doesn't get a response within a timeout period, assume failure and retry or redirect.
 Exponential Backoff: Retry after 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s... to avoid thundering herd problem.
 Circuit Breaker Pattern: After N failures, stop sending requests to a failing service to give it time to recover.
■ KEY TAKEAWAYS
✓ 6 failure types: Crash, Omission, Timing, Byzantine, Network Partition, Fail-Stop.
✓ Byzantine failure is worst — node sends arbitrary/incorrect data (can be malicious).
✓ Fault tolerance strategies: Redundancy, Replication, Checkpointing, Timeouts, Circuit Breaker.
✓ Synchronous replication = strong consistency but slow; Async = fast but possible data loss.

CHAPTER 4
Process Coordination & Synchronization
4.1 The Need for Coordination
In a distributed system, multiple processes run concurrently across different nodes. They must coordinate to: (1)
agree on a shared state, (2) access shared resources without conflict, and (3) handle failures gracefully.
4.2 Logical Clocks — Ordering Events
The Problem
In distributed systems, there's no global clock. Two events on different machines cannot be compared using
wall-clock time due to clock skew (clocks running at different speeds). We need a way to determine the order of
events.
Lamport Timestamps (1978)
Leslie Lamport introduced logical clocks to order events. Each process maintains a counter:
 Rule 1: Before executing an event, increment your counter: L = L + 1
 Rule 2: When sending a message, include your current timestamp.
 Rule 3: When receiving a message with timestamp T: L = max(L, T) + 1
This gives us: If A happened-before B (A → B), then L(A) < L(B). But L(A) < L(B) does NOT necessarily mean A
→ B (can't detect concurrent events).
Vector Clocks
Each process maintains a vector of counters — one per process. Vector clocks can detect concurrent events
(neither A → B nor B → A).
 Each process Pi maintains V[i] = [0, 0, ..., 0] (length N for N processes).
 On internal event: increment V[i][i]
 On send: increment V[i][i], attach V[i] to message
 On receive from Pj: V[i][k] = max(V[i][k], V[j][k]) for all k, then increment V[i][i]
4.3 Mutual Exclusion
Mutual exclusion ensures that only ONE process accesses a critical section (shared resource) at a time. This
prevents race conditions in distributed systems.
Algorithm
How it Works
Messages/Entry
Pros
Cons
Centralized
One coordinator
grants/denies lock
requests
3 messages
Simple, fair

failure, bottleneck

4.4 Deadlock Handling
A deadlock occurs when two or more processes are waiting for each other to release resources, and none can
proceed.
Four Necessary Conditions (Coffman Conditions)
 Mutual Exclusion: At least one resource is held in non-sharable mode.
 Hold and Wait: A process holding resources is waiting for additional resources held by others.
 No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a process.
 Circular Wait: A circular chain of processes exists, each waiting for a resource held by the next.
Deadlock Strategies
 Strategy
 Approach
 Mechanism
 
Prevention
Eliminate one Coffman condition
Total resource ordering; request all
resources at once
Avoidance
Allocate resources only if safe state
maintained
Banker's Algorithm — check if
allocation leads to safe state
Detection
Allow deadlock, detect it, then
recover
Maintain resource allocation graph
(RAG); look for cycles
Recovery
After detection, break the deadlock
Kill a process; preempt (rollback) a
resource; restart
Distributed Deadlock Detection
 Wait-For Graph (WFG): Edge from Pi to Pj means Pi is waiting for Pj to release a resource. A cycle in WFG =
deadlock.
 Chandy-Misra-Haas Algorithm: Probe messages are sent along WFG edges; if a probe returns to the
initiator, a deadlock is detected.

■ KEY TAKEAWAYS
✓ Lamport clocks: L = max(L, received_timestamp) + 1. Capture happened-before, not concurrency.
✓ Vector clocks: detect concurrent events; V[i][k] = max(V[i][k], V[j][k]) on receive.
✓ Mutual exclusion algorithms: Centralized (3 msg), Ricart-Agrawala (2(N-1) msg), Token Ring.
✓ Deadlock = Mutual Exclusion + Hold&Wait; + No Preemption + Circular Wait (ALL 4 needed).
✓ Deadlock handling: Prevention / Avoidance (Banker's) / Detection (WFG) / Recovery.

CHAPTER 5
Parallel Computing vs Distributed Computing
5.1 Parallel Computing
Parallel computing is the simultaneous use of multiple compute resources (processors/cores) to solve a
problem. The problem is divided into smaller parts that execute concurrently on a single machine or tightly
coupled cluster.
 Goal: Speed up computation by doing many things at the same time.
 Memory: Processors typically share memory (shared-memory model).
 Coupling: Tightly coupled — low-latency communication via shared memory bus.
 Example: Matrix multiplication using multi-threading; GPU computing with CUDA; rendering frames in
parallel.
Flynn's Taxonomy (Parallel Architectures)
 Type
 Instruction Streams
Data Streams
Example
SISD
Single
Single
Traditional single-core
CPU
SIMD
Single
Multiple
GPU, vector processors,
AVX instructions
MISD
Multiple
Single
Rare — fault-tolerant flight
computers
MIMD
Multiple
Multiple
Modern multi-core CPUs,
computer clusters
Amdahl's Law
Amdahl's Law predicts the theoretical speedup from parallelizing a program:
Speedup = 1 / (S + (1-S)/N)
Where S = sequential fraction of the program (cannot be parallelized), N = number of processors.
■ EXAMPLE: If 20% of code is sequential (S=0.2) and N=100 processors: Max Speedup = 1 / (0.2 + 0.8/100) =
4.95x. Even with infinite processors, speedup is limited to 1/S = 5x.
5.2 Distributed Computing
Distributed computing uses multiple independent computers (nodes) connected by a network to solve
problems collaboratively. Each node has its own memory and communicates only by passing messages.
 Goal: Achieve scale, fault tolerance, and geographic distribution.
 Memory: Each node has private memory — no shared memory.
 Coupling: Loosely coupled — high-latency network communication.
 Example: Google MapReduce, Apache Spark, blockchain networks.

■ KEY TAKEAWAYS
✓ Parallel: same machine, speed up by simultaneous execution, often shared memory.
✓ Distributed: different machines, communicate by messages, scale + fault tolerance.
✓ Amdahl's Law: speedup limited by sequential fraction S — max = 1/S even with infinite CPUs.
✓ Flynn's Taxonomy: SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD — MIMD = most common today (multi-core CPU).
✓ Key difference: distributed systems have partial failures and no global clock.

CHAPTER 6
CAP Theorem & PACELC Theorem
6.1 CAP Theorem
CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000; formally proven by Gilbert & Lynch, 2002) states that a distributed system
can guarantee AT MOST TWO of the following three properties simultaneously:
C — Consistency
Every read receives the most recent write or an error. All nodes see the same data at
the same time. (Note: NOT the same as ACID consistency.)
A — Availability
Every request receives a non-error response — but it might not contain the most
recent data. The system is always responsive.
P — Partition
Tolerance
The system continues operating even when network partitions (communication
breaks between nodes) occur.
CAP Theorem Triangle
CONSISTENCY

/ \
(CA) / \ (CP)
RDBMS / CAN \ MongoDB
Postgres / ONLY \ HBase
/ PICK 2! \
/ \

(AP) Cassandra, CouchDB, DynamoDB

■ IMPORTANT: In practice, network partitions DO happen (you can't prevent them). So real systems must choose
between CP and AP — CA is not viable for distributed systems over an unreliable network.
6.2 Consistency Models
Consistency is not binary — it's a spectrum. From strongest to weakest:
Strong Consistency
(Linearizability)
After a write completes, all subsequent reads from any node return the new value.
Operations appear instantaneous and ordered. Highest guarantee, highest latency.
Used in: banking, Zookeeper.
Sequential
Consistency
All nodes see operations in the same order, but that order doesn't have to match real
time. Weaker than linearizability.
Causal
Consistency
Causally related operations (A caused B) are seen in order by all nodes. Concurrent
operations may be seen in any order. Used in: distributed collaborative editing.
Eventual
Consistency
If no new updates are made, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value.
No guarantee of when. Used in: DNS, Cassandra, DynamoDB defaults, social media
feeds.
Read-Your-Writes
A process always reads its own latest write. Doesn't guarantee other processes see
it. Used in: user profile updates.
Monotonic Read
Once a process reads a value, it never reads an older value in subsequent reads.
6.3 PACELC Theorem
The PACELC theorem (Daniel Abadi, 2010) extends CAP by also considering the trade-off in the normal case
(no partition):
PACELC = PAC + ELC
 P — Partition: If there's a network partition...
 A — Availability vs C — Consistency: ...choose Availability or Consistency (= CAP).
 E — Else (normal operation, no partition)...
 L — Latency vs C — Consistency: ...choose between low Latency or strong Consistency.

(P→A/C)

(E→L/C)

■ KEY TAKEAWAYS
✓ CAP: Consistency + Availability + Partition Tolerance — can only guarantee 2 of 3.
✓ In real distributed systems: network partitions WILL happen, so choose CP or AP.
✓ CP examples: MongoDB, Zookeeper, HBase. AP examples: Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB.
✓ Consistency spectrum: Strong (Linearizable) > Sequential > Causal > Eventual.
✓ PACELC adds: during normal operation, trade-off is Latency vs Consistency.

CHAPTER 7
Consensus Algorithms: Raft & Paxos
7.1 What is Consensus?
Consensus is the problem of getting multiple nodes in a distributed system to agree on a single value (e.g., who
the leader is, what the next log entry is). This is fundamental to building reliable distributed systems.
 Safety: All correct nodes agree on the SAME value.
 Liveness: Eventually, a value IS decided (the system makes progress).
 FLP Impossibility (1985): It is impossible to achieve consensus in an asynchronous system if even one
process can fail. In practice, systems use timeouts to work around this.
7.2 Paxos — Internal Working
Paxos (Leslie Lamport, 1989) is the foundational consensus algorithm. It tolerates crash failures (not Byzantine).
Roles in Paxos
 Proposer: Proposes a value (initiates consensus).
 Acceptor: Votes on proposals (core of the algorithm). Typically a quorum of N/2+1 acceptors is needed.
 Learner: Learns the chosen value after consensus is reached.
Paxos Phases
Basic Paxos Algorithm Flow
PHASE 1 — PREPARE:
Proposer selects proposal number n and sends Prepare(n) to majority of Acceptors.
Each Acceptor: if n > highest seen, promise to not accept proposals < n,
reply with Promise(n, highest_accepted_value).
PHASE 2 — ACCEPT:
If Proposer receives promises from majority:
- Use highest accepted value from promises (if any), else use own value.
- Send Accept(n, value) to acceptors.
Each Acceptor: if n >= promised, accept the value, send Accepted(n, value).
PHASE 3 — LEARN:
If majority sent Accepted, consensus is reached. Notify Learners.
■ MULTI-PAXOS: Multi-Paxos: Optimizes Paxos for multiple sequential decisions (like a replicated log) by electing
a stable leader, so Phase 1 only needs to happen once, then Phase 2 repeats for each log entry.
7.3 Raft — Internal Working
Raft (Ongaro & Ousterhout, 2014) was designed to be MORE UNDERSTANDABLE than Paxos while being
equivalent in fault tolerance. Used in: etcd, CockroachDB, TiKV, Consul.
Key Concepts
19:
Advanced System Design  |  23CS020
Chitkara University    B.E. CSE 6th Sem
Page 19  |  Exam: 09 April 2026  |  60 Marks  |  60 Minutes
Server States
Each Raft node is in one of 3 states: FOLLOWER (default, receives updates),
CANDIDATE (campaigns for leadership), LEADER (handles all writes and replicates
log).
Term
Raft divides time into terms (logical clock). Each term begins with an election. Terms
are numbered monotonically. If a node sees a higher term, it immediately becomes a
follower.
Log
A replicated log of commands. Leader appends entries and replicates to followers.
Entry is committed when replicated to majority.
Quorum
Majority of nodes (floor(N/2)+1). Any two quorums share at least one member — this
ensures consistency.
Raft — Leader Election
Raft Leader Election
NORMAL STATE: ELECTION TRIGGER:
[Leader] --heartbeat--> [Follower] Follower receives no heartbeat within
[Leader] --heartbeat--> [Follower] election timeout (150-300ms random).
[Leader] --heartbeat--> [Follower] Follower becomes CANDIDATE.
ELECTION PROCESS:
1. Candidate increments term, votes for itself.
2. Sends RequestVote(term, candidateId, lastLogIndex, lastLogTerm) to all.
3. Each node grants vote if: (a) hasn't voted this term, AND
(b) candidate's log is at least as up-to-date as voter's log.
4. Candidate with majority votes becomes LEADER.
5. New leader immediately sends heartbeats to assert authority.
Raft — Log Replication
 Step 1: Client sends command to Leader.
 Step 2: Leader appends command to its log as a new entry.
 Step 3: Leader sends AppendEntries RPC to all followers.
 Step 4: When majority of followers acknowledge, leader COMMITS the entry.
 Step 5: Leader applies to state machine and responds to client. Next heartbeat informs followers of commit.

■ KEY TAKEAWAYS
✓ Consensus: agree on a single value — Safety (all agree same value) + Liveness (progress).
✓ FLP Impossibility: cannot solve consensus in async system with even 1 possible failure.
✓ Paxos phases: Prepare → Promise → Accept → Accepted. Need quorum (majority) at each phase.
✓ Raft states: Follower → Candidate → Leader. Uses terms and heartbeats for leader election.
✓ Raft: majority commit, randomized election timeout prevents split votes.
✓ Both Raft and Paxos tolerate f failures with 2f+1 nodes.

CHAPTER 8
Leader Election & Failure Detection
8.1 Why Leader Election?
Many distributed algorithms require a coordinator or leader — a single process that has special responsibilities
(e.g., accepting writes, coordinating transactions, deciding log entries). When a leader fails, the system must
elect a new leader automatically to continue functioning.
8.2 Leader Election Algorithms
Bully Algorithm
The Bully Algorithm assumes each node has a unique ID. The node with the highest ID becomes leader.
Bully Algorithm
SCENARIO: Node 3 detects Node 5 (current leader) has failed.
Step 1: Node 3 sends ELECTION message to all nodes with higher IDs (4, 5, 6, 7).
Step 2: If no response within timeout => Node 3 declares itself leader.
If Node 4, 6, or 7 responds with OK:
Step 3: Responding node starts its OWN election (sends ELECTION to nodes above it).
Step 4: Node with highest ID that is alive wins, sends COORDINATOR message to all.
Messages: O(N^2) in worst case. Advantage: Simple. Disadvantage: High message overhead.
Ring Election Algorithm
Processes are arranged in a logical ring. Each process knows its neighbour.
Ring Election Algorithm
SCENARIO: Node 2 detects leader failure, starts election.
[1] --> [2*] --> [3] --> [4] --> [5] --> [6] --> [1] (* = initiator)
Step 1: Node 2 sends ELECTION(2) to neighbour Node 3.
Step 2: Each node receiving ELECTION(id): if id > own id, forward; else replace with own
id.
Step 3: Message travels around ring. When it returns to Node 2 with its OWN id => winner.
Step 4: Node 2 sends COORDINATOR(2) around ring to announce leadership.
Messages: O(N). Advantage: fewer messages. Disadvantage: N messages just to detect winner.
Raft Leader Election (Recap)
As covered in Chapter 7: uses randomized timeouts (150-300ms), terms, and RequestVote RPCs. Node with
most up-to-date log and majority votes wins.

8.3 Failure Detection
Failure detection is the mechanism by which nodes in a distributed system determine whether another node has
crashed or is unreachable. This is challenging because a slow node looks the same as a crashed node.
Heartbeat Protocol
 Mechanism: Each node periodically sends a 'heartbeat' message (I'm alive) to its neighbours or a central
monitor.
 Timeout: If no heartbeat is received within a timeout T, the node is suspected to have failed.
 Problem: False positives — a healthy but slow/overloaded node might miss heartbeat deadlines.
Phi Accrual Failure Detector (Cassandra)
Instead of binary (alive/dead), outputs a continuous suspicion value (phi) — higher phi = more likely failed.
Application sets its own threshold based on tolerance for false positives.
SWIM Protocol (Scalable Weakly-consistent Infection-style Membership)
Used in modern systems (Consul, Hashicorp Serf). Instead of pinging the suspected node directly, a random set
of other nodes ping it. This avoids overloading a slow node.
 Indirect probing: Node A suspects Node B. A asks Nodes C, D, E to ping B. If none get a response, B is
suspected failed.
 Gossip dissemination: Failure information is spread via gossip protocol — each node periodically shares its
membership list with a random neighbour.
Gossip Protocol
A gossip protocol (epidemic protocol) is a way to disseminate information in a distributed system:
 Each node periodically selects a random neighbour and exchanges its knowledge (membership list, failure
info).
 Information spreads exponentially fast — like a rumour spreading through a crowd.
 Convergence time: O(log N) rounds to reach all N nodes.
 Advantages: Highly scalable, resilient to failures, no single point of failure.
 Used in: Cassandra (gossip for cluster membership), Amazon S3, Riak.

■ KEY TAKEAWAYS
✓ Bully Algorithm: highest ID wins; O(N^2) messages. Ring: O(N) messages; loops around ring.
✓ Raft leader election: randomized timeout, term numbers, most up-to-date log wins majority vote.
✓ Heartbeat: simple but binary (alive/dead). Phi Accrual: continuous suspicion value.
✓ SWIM: indirect probing to avoid false positives from slow nodes.
✓ Gossip Protocol: epidemic spread of info; O(log N) convergence; highly fault-tolerant.

CHAPTER ✓
QUICK REVISION SHEET — Exam Ready!
One-Liner Facts for MCQs
Latency: Time for ONE request. Throughput = requests
PER SECOND.
Availability formula: Uptime / (Uptime + Downtime).
99.999% = 5.26 min downtime/year.
Functional req.: WHAT system does. Non-functional =
HOW WELL it does it.
Microservices: Independent deployable services, each
owning its own data.
Distributed system: Independent computers appearing as
one; communicate by messages.
No Global Clock: Each node has own clock; use
Lamport/Vector clocks for ordering.
Lamport rule: L = max(L, received_T) + 1. Captures
happened-before, not concurrency.
Vector clocks: Can detect CONCURRENT events; Lamport
clocks CANNOT.
Crash failure: Node stops completely. Byzantine = node
sends WRONG/malicious data.
Fault tolerance: Redundancy + Replication + Checkpointing
+ Circuit Breaker.
Mutual exclusion: Only ONE process in critical section at a
time.
Ricart-Agrawala: 2(N-1) messages; all nodes must OK the
request. No central coordinator.
Token Ring: Only token holder enters CS. Lost token =
problem.
Deadlock 4 conds.: Mutual Exclusion + Hold&Wait; + No
Preemption + Circular Wait.
Banker's Algorithm: Deadlock AVOIDANCE — checks safe
state before resource allocation.
Deadlock Detection: Maintain WFG; cycle in WFG =
deadlock.
Parallel computing: Same machine, shared memory, speed
up computation.
Distributed comp.: Different machines, message passing,
fault tolerance + scale.
Amdahl's Law: Speedup = 1/(S + (1-S)/N); limited by
sequential fraction S.
CAP — C: Consistency: all nodes see same data. C in CAP
!= ACID C.
CAP — A: Availability: every request gets non-error
response.
CAP — P: Partition Tolerance: system works despite
network splits.
CAP choice: Must pick CP or AP (P is mandatory in real
distributed systems).
CP examples: Zookeeper, MongoDB, HBase,
CockroachDB.
AP examples: Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB, Riak.
Eventual consistency: All replicas converge eventually if no
new updates. Weakest model.
Linearizability: Strongest consistency. Operations appear
instantaneous and ordered.
PACELC: PAC (partition) + ELC (else: normal case latency
vs consistency).
FLP Impossibility: Cannot guarantee consensus in async
system if even 1 node may fail.
Paxos roles: Proposer, Acceptor, Learner. Need majority
(quorum) to decide.
Paxos phases: Phase 1: Prepare/Promise. Phase 2:
Accept/Accepted.
Raft states: Follower (default) -> Candidate (election) ->
Leader.
Raft terms: Monotonically increasing. Higher term => more
recent. Node with higher term wins.
Raft election: Random timeout (150-300ms). Most
up-to-date log + majority votes wins.
Raft commit: Entry committed when replicated to
MAJORITY of nodes.
Bully Algorithm: Highest ID node wins. O(N^2) messages.
Ring Election: Message circulates ring; highest ID wins.
O(N) messages.
Heartbeat: Periodic 'I'm alive' message. No heartbeat in
timeout = suspected failed.

SWIM Protocol: Indirect probing for failure detection; avoids

Quorum: Majority = floor(N/2)+1. Any 2 quorums share at

2f+1 nodes: Need 2f+1 nodes to tolerate f crash failures
(Paxos/Raft).
3f+1 nodes: Need 3f+1 nodes to tolerate f BYZANTINE
failures (PBFT).
